coredata.dc:title: Effect of pre-oxidation on the properties of aluminide coating layers formed on Ti alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Y.G.
Zhou, W.
Qin, Q.D.
Liang, Y.H.
Jiang, Q.C.
coredata.dc:description: The aluminized and pre-oxidized+aluminized Ti alloys, oxidized at 600°C in air for 50h, are studied by SEM and XRD, respectively. The results show that before oxidation the aluminide coating is composed of Al3Ti phase, but the pre-oxidation+aluminide coating consists of Al2TiO5, Al5Ti3 and Al3Ti phases. After oxidation they are both composed of Al2O3 and TiO2. A comparison study has been carried out between two samples to determine the influence of pre-oxidation upon the properties of aluminizing and oxidation resistance. The oxidation kinetics shows that the weight change of pre-oxidized+aluminized sample is less than that of the aluminized sample. The whole thickness of the pre-oxidation+aluminide scale is about half of that of the aluminide scale. The pre-oxidation affects not only the aluminizing but also the adherence between the scale and the substrate. So the pre-oxidation+aluminizing is an effective method to improve the oxidation resistance of Ti alloy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ti alloy
Aluminide coating
Pre-oxidation
Oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pre-oxidation on aluminizing kinetics
Effect of pre-oxidation on oxidation resistance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic oxidation kinetics of Ti alloy with aluminizing, pre-oxidation+aluminizing and uncoating at 600°C for 50h.
XRD patterns of scale formed on the Ti alloy with (a) pre-oxidation+aluminizing and (b) aluminizing.
Surface morphologies of Ti alloy with (a) pre-oxidation+aluminizing and (b) aluminizing.
Surface morphologies of Ti alloy with (a) pre-oxidation+aluminizing, (b) aluminizing and (c) uncoated after 50h cyclic oxidation at 600°C.
Cross-section microstructures of Ti alloy with (a) pre-oxidation+aluminizing, (b) aluminizing and (c) uncoating after 50h cyclic oxidation at 600°C.

article.ce:title: Effect of pre-oxidation on the properties of aluminide coating layers formed on Ti alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials of Department of Ministry of Education and Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University at Nanling Campus, No. 142 Renmin Street, Changchun 130025, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors. Tel.: +86 431 509 4481 (Y.G. Zhao)/+86 431 509 5592 (Q.C. Jiang); fax: +86 431 509 4481 (Y.G. Zhao)/+86 431 509 5592 (Q.C. Jiang).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aluminized and pre-oxidized+aluminized Ti alloys, oxidized at 600°C in air for 50h, are studied by SEM and XRD, respectively. The results show that before oxidation the aluminide coating is composed of Al3Ti phase, but the pre-oxidation+aluminide coating consists of Al2TiO5, Al5Ti3 and Al3Ti phases. After oxidation they are both composed of Al2O3 and TiO2. A comparison study has been carried out between two samples to determine the influence of pre-oxidation upon the properties of aluminizing and oxidation resistance. The oxidation kinetics shows that the weight change of pre-oxidized+aluminized sample is less than that of the aluminized sample. The whole thickness of the pre-oxidation+aluminide scale is about half of that of the aluminide scale. The pre-oxidation affects not only the aluminizing but also the adherence between the scale and the substrate. So the pre-oxidation+aluminizing is an effective method to improve the oxidation resistance of Ti alloy.

ce:section.ce:para: Ti alloy is often used in heat-conducting tubes in vapour generators and in other components in aviation and nuclear reactors. To work safely for prolonged periods in high-temperature and high-pressure environments, Ti alloy and its products are required to have excellent oxidation resistance. Therefore improving the oxidation resistance of Ti alloy is always a subject of concern. Many methods have been used to modify the surface properties of Ti alloy in order to improve the oxidation resistance. Improvement of oxidation resistance by addition of alloying element such as Nb, Al or Si [1–5] is not enough for practical application and alloying element may have a deleterious effect on the mechanical properties of material. Several studies on the surface treatments have been performed to improve the oxidation resistance, which includes as follow: MCrAlY overlay coating [6,7], aluminizing [8,9], silicide/ceramics [10,11] and pre-oxidation at low oxygen pressure [12,13]. However, none of them seems sufficiently successful.

Aluminized coating is widely used as a coating method for high-temperature materials because of its practical advantages over other coating method. The pack aluminizing of Ti alloy produces basically Al3Ti coating. However, during cyclic oxidation under thermal stress, the Al3Ti is easily spalled off due to its brittle nature. So how to better improve the compatibility with substrate is a hotspot. The addition of a small amount of Nb or Cr in the Ti alloy can improve the oxidation resistance, which has changed the crystal structure of Al3Ti from low symmetry DO22 to high symmetry LI2 [14]. Someone has pointed out that the pre-oxidation has much effect on the aluminizing; however, the study of the effects of pre-oxidation on the aluminizing process and the oxidation resistance of Ti alloy are rather limited.

In the present work, pack aluminizing and pre-oxidation+aluminizing are investigated with Ti alloy. To examine the effect of pre-oxidation on the aluminizing behavior of Ti alloy, two different aluminizings are tested by cyclic oxidation at 600°C in air.

The nominal chemical composition of the Ti alloy used in this experiment is shown in Table 1 . The Ti–6Al–4V alloy is prepared by cutting the rolled plate into coupons of approximate dimension 9mm×9mm×3.5mm, grinding their surface to a 2500-grit finish, and ultrasonically cleaning with ethanol.

The pre-oxidation process is made as follows: the substrate is placed in an aluminide crucible, and then put the crucible in an electric furnace which is heated to 1000°C, a 100mlmin−1 flow of argon is passed through the furnace to provide an inert environment. When finishing the pre-oxidation after 4h, the samples are allowed to furnace cool down to ambient temperature, then getting out of the samples and shutting off the argon.

The halide-activated pack cementation process is used to deposit aluminide onto the Ti alloy coupons. Pack chemistry for aluminide coating is as follows: 5wt.% Al, 94.5wt.% Y2O3 and 0.5wt.% NH4Cl. The pack mixtures are weighed and then mixed sufficiently, subsequently the substrate samples and pack materials are placed in an aluminide crucible. The crucible is placed into an electric tube furnace which has been heated to 1000°C and kept the temperature for 5h. From the beginning of the furnace heated up to 950°C, a 100mlmin−1 flow of argon is passed through the furnace to provide a protective environment. On completion of a coating deposition, the pack is allowed to furnace cool down to ambient temperature, taking out of the samples then turning off the argon. After diffusion, the samples are removed from the pack and ultrasonically cleaned to remove any loosely embedded pack materials.

In order to evaluate the pre-oxidation on the aluminizing and the oxidation resistance of the Ti alloys, cyclic oxidation test is carried out in static air at 600°C for a period of 50h. The weight change is monitored initially every 2h (five cycles) in each test with a view to observe any rapid weight change and subsequently every 5h, i.e., holding the samples in the required temperature zone for 5h and then taking out and cooling down to room temperature for 20min in air. The sample weights are measured to 10−4 g at each cyclic until the end of the respective tests.
After cyclic oxidation, the samples are analyzed using optical metallography (OM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) equipped with energy disperse X-ray analysis (EDAX).

Fig. 1 shows the weight change as a function of exposure time for Ti alloy with aluminide coating, pre-oxidation+aluminide coating and uncoating. In order to understand the effect of the pre-oxidation, the oxidation data of pre-oxidized+aluminized samples are compared with those of aluminized and uncoated samples. It is very clear that the oxidation of the aluminized samples and pre-oxidized+aluminized samples follow quasi-parabolic kinetics, but the uncoated samples follows a line kinetics before spallation. Comparing with the uncoated sample, the aluminide coating and pre-oxidation+aluminide coating both provide a good oxidation resistance and the pre-oxidation+aluminide coating is much better. To the uncoated samples, the scale begins to widely spall after 25h. The good properties of the aluminide coating and pre-oxidation+aluminide coating are attributed to the formation of a protective Al2O3 layer

In order to investigate the effect of pre-oxidation on the aluminizing behavior, the X-ray diffraction is carried to compare with the phases of the two different aluminizings. It can be seen from Fig. 2 that Al5Ti3, Al3Ti and Al2TiO5 phases appear in the pre-oxidation+aluminide coating, but there is only Al3Ti phase in the aluminide coating.

Fig. 3 shows the aluminide surface morphology of Ti alloy with and without pre-oxidation at 1000°C. From the surface morphology of the two coatings, it can be seen that the particles are fine, compact and homogenizing, but there are many Y2O3 particles in the aluminide coating. Fig. 4 shows the aluminide surface morphology of Ti alloys with and without pre-oxidation after 50h cyclic oxidation at 600°C. There is not much change comparing with before oxidation, but as can be seen from Fig. 4, a much number of small particles lie in the aluminide scale, and the XRD shows the small particles predominantly consisting of Y2O3, but only a few of them in the pre-oxidation+aluminide scale. However, a lot of spallations have been found in the uncoated Ti alloy. Fig. 5 shows the cross-sectional microstructures of scales of the three samples after 50h oxidation at 600°C. Comparing with the pre-oxidized+aluminized samples, the thickness of the aluminide scale is much thicker (about two times) than that of the pre-oxidation+aluminide scale, and many cracks appear in the aluminide scale but those cannot be found in the pre-oxidation+aluminide scale. It indicates that the pre-oxidation+aluminide coating provides a good ductility and adherence between the scale and the substrate. Due to the crack showing, the oxygen can easily diffuse through the scale and dissolve in the substrate, so the aluminide coating provides a limited protective coating. In the cross-sectional uncoated Ti alloy also the cracks appear. It can be concluded that the pre-oxidation affects greatly the oxidation resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pre-oxidation on aluminizing kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: In the present investigation, only a single Al3Ti phase forms during aluminized Ti alloy at 1000°C for 5h, but two phases are formed, such as Al5Ti3, Al3Ti and Al2TiO5, in the pre-oxidation+aluminide coating and the thickness of the aluminide coating is much thicker. Differences in the aspects of coating structure and aluminizing kinetics between samples with and without pre-oxidation can be explained on the basis of effect of pre-oxidation. The aluminide coating formed on pure Ti alloy grows predominantly by the inward diffusion of Al. During aluminizing, active Al atoms react and interdiffuse with Ti alloy, producing Al3Ti layer. Zhou et al. [14,15] have investigated that the aluminide coating formed on pure Ti alloy is duplex in structure, with a thin inner layer and a thick outer layer, the compositions of the inner and outer layers correspond to Al2Ti and Al3Ti, respectively. But, in the investigation the XRD does not show the Al2Ti phase, this may be due to their quantity being less than the detection limit of the X-ray diffractometer. However, the pre-oxidized Ti alloy has formed a continuous and dense TiO2 +Al2O3 coating layer mixture, which will make the active Al atoms not greatly diffuse through the layer to form the Al3Ti phase but the Al5Ti3 phase and react with TiO2 and Al2O3 to form the Al2TiO5 phase. Due to the barrier of the pre-oxidation coating layer, the number of active Al atoms diffused through the layer decreases greatly. For the pre-oxidized Ti alloy, the formation of Al5Ti3 layer is suppressed because after the thin pre-oxidation layer forms, the growth rate of the coating is controlled by solid-state diffusion of active Al atoms in coating layer. Through computing the aluminide weight changes of samples with and without pre-oxidation are, respectively, 6.83252 and 7.96784mg/cm2.

Much of Y2O3 contains in the packing mixtures, when it is heated up to 1000°C, a great deal Y2O3 diffuses in the aluminide coating, but there is only a few of them in the pre-oxidation+aluminide coating. The pre-oxidation also decreases the diffusion of Y2O3 in the coating due to the barrier of the pre-oxidation coating layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pre-oxidation on oxidation resistance
ce:section.ce:para:
The oxidation test data show that the pre-oxidation+aluminized Ti alloy has much better oxidation resistance than the aluminized Ti alloy. According to Fig. 1, the cyclic oxidation kinetics of Ti alloy with aluminizing and pre-oxidation+aluminizing follow parabolic law, the parabolic constants
K ′
p
(weight gain2 versus time) are, respectively, 0.0054 and 0.0038mg2 cm−4 h−1. As shown in Fig. 5, the aluminide coating layer after 50h oxidation at 600°C shows many severe cracks. The reason is that the Al3Ti is a low symmetry DO22, during cyclic oxidation under thermal stress, and easily spalls with cracks. Oxygen can diffuse to the substrate through the cracks and react with the substrate to form a non-protective TiO2, which results in a high oxidation rate.

The pre-oxidized+aluminized Ti alloy shows much lower oxidation rate and less cracks in the scale, XRD shows the pre-oxidation+aluminide coating is not composed of Al3Ti phase which has a tetragonal DO22 structure that has low symmetry and thus offer poor ductility, but Al5Ti3 and Al2TiO5 phases which can improve ductility by having slip systems at high temperature. In the present investigation, the pre-oxidation improves the ductility of coating by modifying the crystal structure of aluminide coating layer, resulting in a better compatibility with substrate and improving the spallation resistance. Many of the studies have been conducted on the transformation of the crystal structure through the addition of ternary alloying elements to improve TiAl3 ductility. Ternary alloying elements such as Ni, Fe, Mn and Cr are confirmed to change the crystal structure of TiAl3 from low symmetry DO22 to high symmetry LI2 [15–19]. Owing to forming the LI2 structure and resultant coating with few cracks, few oxygen atoms diffuse to the surface of pre-oxidized Ti alloy through the cracks in the scale, which will decrease greatly the oxidation rate of Ti alloy. Thus, the pre-oxidation+aluminide Ti alloy has much better oxidation resistance than the aluminized Ti alloy.

From the study on the aluminide coating with and without pre-oxidation, the following conclusions are obtained:
1.
The pre-oxidation has a great effect on the aluminizing process. Three phases, Al5Ti3, Al3Ti and Al2TiO5, are formed in the pre-oxidation+aluminide coating, but only one phase Al3Ti is found in the aluminide coating, and the thickness of the aluminide coating formed on Ti alloy is much thicker.
2.
After 50h cyclic oxidation at 600°C in air, many cracks appear in the aluminide scale; however, it cannot be found in the pre-oxidation+aluminide scale. The pre-oxidized+aluminized sample has much better oxidation resistance and spallation resistance than the aluminized sample.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province in PR China (contract no. 20020628-1).
